county_metadata.record_losses: February 17, 1865: Sherman's army burned Columbia, destroying the courthouse and most county records. However, equity and probate records had been removed before the fire and survived. Conveyance (deed) records were lost and had to be rebuilt from 1865 forward. Post-1865 conveyance books survive on 118 microfilm reels. Estate papers (1787-1909) on 104 microfilm reels survived. Court of Common Pleas judgment rolls survive from 1856.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1785 from Camden District. Small portion went to Kershaw County in 1791. Named for the 'rich land' soil quality. SC capital moved to Columbia in 1786, making Richland the seat of state government. Converted from district to county in 1868.
county_metadata.research_tips: This is a BURNED COUNTY but NOT a total loss — equity and probate records survived because they were removed before Sherman's fire. Start with the surviving estate papers (1787-1909) on 104 microfilm reels at SCDAH. Conveyance books 1865-1951 (118 reels) are the rebuilt post-fire deed records. State land grant plats (1784-1868) at archivesindex.sc.gov are CRITICAL for pre-1865 land research. Will Transcripts (1782-1855) at SCDAH include Richland entries. Equity bills (1793-1868) survived and are invaluable. The Walker Local History Room at Richland Library holds collections emphasizing Midlands counties. The South Caroliniana Library at USC has extensive manuscript collections. Columbia's status as state capital means legislative and state government records supplement county losses. Check Camden District (Kershaw County) for 1785-1791 records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Post-fire conveyance books 1865-1951 on microfilm. Modern records available through county portal. Pre-1865 deed records destroyed in fire.
county_metadata.heritage_book: Richland County, SC: A History by Edwin L. Green

source_profiles.repository: FamilySearch / SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: 104 microfilm reels. Index covers 1787-1950. These records SURVIVED the 1865 fire because they were removed before Sherman's troops arrived. One of the most valuable surviving Richland record sets.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Safely removed before the 1865 fire. Complete from 1787.

source_profiles.repository: FamilySearch / SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: 118 microfilm reels. Post-fire reconstructed deed records. Pre-1865 conveyance records were destroyed.

source_profiles.source_name: South Carolina Will Transcripts — Richland District
source_profiles.repository: SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Part of statewide will transcription project. Searchable by name with images. Critical for pre-fire Richland research.
source_profiles.survival_notes: State-level compilation survived independently of county records.

source_profiles.repository: SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Equity court records that survived the fire. Equity cases reference property transactions, boundary disputes, and family relationships — partial substitute for destroyed deed records.

source_profiles.repository: SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: State-level plat records for land grants. CRITICAL substitute for destroyed county deed records. Part of the 51,055-image statewide collection.
source_profiles.survival_notes: State-held records — not affected by county courthouse fire.

source_profiles.source_name: Richland County Court of Common Pleas Judgment Rolls, 1856-1951
source_profiles.repository: SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: 18 microfilm reels. Post-1856 judgment rolls survive.

county_metadata.record_losses: No known major record losses. Records intact from county formation in 1895. Parent county Edgefield never burned, so pre-formation records are also intact.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed February 25, 1895 (some sources say 1896) from northern and eastern portions of Edgefield County. Only county formed by action of the SC Constitutional Convention. Named for the Saluda River. Pre-1895 records are in Edgefield County, which has outstanding record survival.
county_metadata.research_tips: Saluda is a young county (1895) so pre-formation research requires checking Edgefield County records, which are among the best-preserved in SC (Edgefield famously NEVER burned). Edgefield records date to the colonial period. SC Magazine of Ancestral Research (SCMAR) has published Edgefield deed abstracts covering the Saluda area. County mapping system at saludacounty.sc.gov provides some property access. Saluda County Library has limited genealogical resources. The Saluda County Museum has local history materials.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Deeds and property records via county services portal from county formation (1895). Pre-1895 records in Edgefield County.

source_profiles.repository: Saluda County Register of Deeds
source_profiles.coverage_notes: County formation forward. Pre-1895 records in Edgefield County.
source_profiles.survival_notes: All records survive from county formation.

source_profiles.source_name: Edgefield County Deed Records (pre-Saluda formation area)
source_profiles.repository: Edgefield County Clerk of Court / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Pre-Saluda-formation deed records for the area. Edgefield never burned — outstanding record preservation from colonial period.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Edgefield records are among SC's best preserved (never burned).

source_profiles.source_name: South Carolina Will Transcripts — Edgefield District (includes Saluda area)
source_profiles.repository: SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Part of statewide will transcription project. Includes wills from what would become Saluda County.

county_metadata.record_losses: No known major record losses. Excellent record survival from 1785 forward.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1785 from Ninety-Six District. Named for the Spartan Regiment, a Revolutionary War militia unit. Part of Pinckney District 1791-1799. Converted from district to county in 1868. Cherokee County carved from Spartanburg (plus York and Union) in 1897.
county_metadata.research_tips: Spartanburg has outstanding early deed records. Albert Bruce Pruitt's 'Spartanburg County Deed Abstracts, Books A-T, 1785-1827' is the essential starting resource. Online deed search at search.spartanburgdeeds.com covers modern records (1983+). FamilySearch has conveyance books 1785-1911 with indexes 1785-1919, plus real estate mortgages 1872-1907. The Pinckney District Chapter of SCGS covers Spartanburg, Cherokee, and Union counties. Spartanburg County Public Libraries have genealogy collections. Wofford College Library has regional historical materials. The Hub City Writers Project has published local histories. Cross-reference with Chester, Fairfield, Greenville, Laurens, and Union for connected families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Online portal for modern records (1983+). FamilySearch has historical deed records 1785-1911. Indexes available 1785-1919.
county_metadata.heritage_book: Spartanburg County, South Carolina, Deed Abstracts, Books A-T, 1785-1827 by Albert Bruce Pruitt

source_profiles.source_name: Spartanburg County Deed Abstracts, Books A-T, 1785-1827
source_profiles.repository: Published / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Albert Bruce Pruitt's comprehensive abstracts of the earliest deed books. Essential starting point for Spartanburg land research.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Published abstracts of complete early records.

source_profiles.repository: FamilySearch / SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Conveyance books with indexes covering 1785-1919. 126 years of deed records on microfilm.

source_profiles.source_name: Spartanburg County Register of Deeds Online
source_profiles.repository: Spartanburg County Register of Deeds
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Online portal for modern records (1983+). Free search.

source_profiles.repository: FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Index to mortgage books 1872-1903. Mortgage records on microfilm.

county_metadata.parent_county: Camden District (via Clarendon, Claremont, and Salem counties)
county_metadata.record_losses: November 27, 1801: Fire at the residence of Sumter District Clerk of Court John Horan in Stateburg destroyed probate records, deeds, and records of three predecessor counties (Clarendon, Claremont, Salem). Pre-1801 records virtually non-existent at the county level. Rebuilt records begin 1801.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1798 (effective 1800) from Camden District, consolidating Clarendon, Claremont, and Salem counties. Named for General Thomas Sumter ('The Gamecock'). Clarendon County re-created from Sumter in 1857. Lee County carved from Sumter in 1902. Converted from district to county in 1868.
county_metadata.research_tips: This is a BURNED COUNTY — the 1801 fire at Clerk Horan's home in Stateburg predates the Civil War burns and destroyed records of Sumter AND its three predecessor counties. Start with post-1801 conveyance records (110 microfilm reels, 1801-1948) at SCDAH. Plat books (1802-1949, 12 reels) parallel the deed records. Will books and estates (1800-1963) were rebuilt. State land grant plats at archivesindex.sc.gov provide some pre-1801 substitute. Check Camden District records for the 1785-1800 period. The Sumter County Museum has local materials. The Sumter Genealogical Society is active. For the Stateburg area, Reconstruction-era Freedmen's Bureau records are valuable. Church of the Holy Cross (Episcopal, Stateburg, 1852) has pre-fire era records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Conveyance books 1801-1949 (110 microfilm reels); plat books 1802-1949 (12 reels). Pre-1801 records destroyed in fire. County website provides office information; records on microfilm at SCDAH.
county_metadata.heritage_book: Some South Carolina Genealogical Records by Janie Revill (includes Sumter); History of Sumter County, South Carolina by Anne King Gregorie (1954)

source_profiles.repository: FamilySearch / SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: 110 microfilm reels. Rebuilt after the 1801 fire. Bulk coverage 1801-1926 with additional records through 1949.

source_profiles.repository: FamilySearch / SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: 12 microfilm reels of plats. Parallel to deed records and essential for land research.

source_profiles.source_name: Sumter County Will Books and Estates, 1800-1963
source_profiles.repository: FamilySearch / SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Index and file books 1800-1963. Rebuilt probate records after 1801 fire.

source_profiles.repository: SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: State-level plat records for land grants. Only pre-1801 land documentation available for the Sumter area. Part of the 51,055-image statewide collection.
source_profiles.survival_notes: State-held records — not affected by county fire.

source_profiles.source_name: Sumter County Probate Records — Real Estate, Sales, Appointments, Inventories
source_profiles.repository: FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Comprehensive post-fire probate records including inventories, appraisements, and sales records.

county_metadata.record_losses: No known major record losses. Excellent record continuity from 1785 forward.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1785 from Ninety-Six District. Named for Union Church, where early settlers of different denominations worshiped together. Converted from district to county in 1868. Cherokee County carved from Union (plus York and Spartanburg) in 1897.
county_metadata.research_tips: Union has excellent record survival with conveyance books going back to 1778 (predating county formation). Brent Holcomb's 'Union County, South Carolina Deed Abstracts' (5 volumes, published by SCMAR) are the essential starting resource. FamilySearch has conveyance books 1778-1921, plat books (1800s-1974), and mortgages (1872-1923). Winthrop College Archives in Rock Hill holds genealogies of Union families. The Pinckney District Chapter of SCGS covers Union along with Spartanburg and Cherokee. Union County Museum has local materials. Check Ninety-Six District records for the overlap period.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Conveyance books 1778-1921 on microfilm; plat books 1800s-1974; mortgages 1872-1923. County website provides department information. Detailed records available through FamilySearch and on-site.
county_metadata.heritage_book: Union County, South Carolina Deed Abstracts by Brent Holcomb (SCMAR, 5 volumes)

source_profiles.repository: FamilySearch / SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: General index 1785-1913. Conveyance books going back to 1778 — predating county formation. Exceptional early coverage.

source_profiles.source_name: Union County Deed Abstracts (Brent Holcomb, 5 volumes)
source_profiles.repository: SC Magazine of Ancestral Research (SCMAR)
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Five-volume comprehensive deed abstract series by Brent Holcomb. Essential reference for Union County land research.

source_profiles.repository: FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Index 1803-1974. Plat books from the early 1800s through 1974.

source_profiles.repository: FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: General index 1872-1969. Mortgage records.

county_metadata.record_losses: No known major record losses. Court records date from 1806 (2 years after district formation). Records intact.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1804 as a district from Georgetown District. Named for King William III of England. Converted from district to county in 1868. Small area went to Florence County in 1888. Court records start 1806, two years after formation.
county_metadata.research_tips: For pre-1804 records, search Georgetown District (formed 1769). Georgetown District records are partially burned (antebellum records destroyed in 1865), so pre-1804 Williamsburg-area records may be affected. Court records begin 1806 on microfilm at SCDAH. FamilySearch has some digitized collections. The Williamsburg Historical Museum in Kingstree has local materials. State land grants (1719-1786) at Register of Mesne Conveyance in Charleston and SCDAH cover the colonial period. The county's Lowcountry plantation heritage means Freedmen's Bureau records and enslaved persons databases are relevant.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Limited online portal. Primary access through microfilm at SCDAH. County website provides office information.

source_profiles.source_name: Williamsburg County Court Records on Microfilm
source_profiles.repository: FamilySearch / SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Court records available on microfilm through SCDAH. Records begin 1806, two years after district formation.

source_profiles.source_name: South Carolina State Land Grants (Georgetown District area), 1719-1786
source_profiles.repository: SC Department of Archives and History / Register of Mesne Conveyance, Charleston
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Colonial-era land grants at the state level. Covers the area that became Williamsburg District. Records at Register of Mesne Conveyance in Charleston and SCDAH.

source_profiles.repository: FamilySearch / SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate records from district formation. Pre-1804 probate under Georgetown District.

county_metadata.record_losses: No known major record losses. Excellent record survival from 1785 forward. York has among the most complete and accessible county records in SC.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1785 from Camden District as one of seven counties (York, Chester, Fairfield, Lancaster, Richland, Claremont, Clarendon). Part of Pinckney District 1791-1800. County seat originally York Court House, incorporated as Yorkville in 1841. Converted from district to county in 1868. Cherokee County carved from York (plus Union and Spartanburg) in 1897.
county_metadata.research_tips: York is a premier genealogical county with outstanding record access across multiple channels. FamilySearch has deed records 1785-1950 online. The Historical Center of York County (part of Culture & Heritage Museums, chmuseums.org) has deeds on microfilm 1785-1977. Online deed search at search.yorkdeeds.com covers 1979-present. Deed abstracts for books A-E (1786-1801) are available. Will Transcripts (1782-1855) at SCDAH include York entries. York County Library branches are FamilySearch Affiliate Libraries. Mortgage book indexes cover 1872-1950. York was a significant Catawba Nation territory and Scots-Irish settlement area — church records (Associate Reformed Presbyterian, Bethesda, Old Nation Ford) are valuable. The county borders NC (Mecklenburg/Charlotte area) so cross-border migration research is important.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Online portal covers 1979-present. FamilySearch has 1785-1950. Historical Center of York County has microfilm 1785-1977. Three-tier access system provides coverage from county formation to present.
county_metadata.heritage_book: York County deed abstracts and historical works (multiple titles through Yorkville Historical Society)

source_profiles.source_name: York County Deed Records on FamilySearch, 1785-1950
source_profiles.repository: FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed abstracts books A-E (1786-1801) plus comprehensive deed records through 1950. Online access through FamilySearch.

source_profiles.source_name: York County Register of Deeds Online
source_profiles.repository: York County Register of Deeds
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Online portal for modern records (1979+). Free search.

source_profiles.repository: Historical Center of York County (Culture & Heritage Museums)
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Complete microfilm collection covering 192 years of deed records. In-person access at the Historical Center's search room.
